comment_text.sn1.37:1.2: This discourse is the same as the first section of DN 20.
comment_text.sn1.37:1.4: The Pure Abodes (suddhāvāsā) are realms into which only nonreturners can be reborn. They are reckoned as five: Aviha, Atappa, Sudassa, Sudassı̄, and Akaniṭṭha.
comment_text.sn1.37:7.1: Read khīla (“post”) rather than khila (“barrenness”).
comment_text.sn1.37:7.2: A village (Bu Pj 2:3.8) or royal compound (Bu Pc 83:1.3.56.1) was marked with a “boundary-post” (indakhīla). It symbolized a fixed and immovable point, either in a good sense (SN 56.39:4.1) or, as here, an obstacle. The Sanskrit indrakīla is a two-foot long iron bolt securing a fort, presumably fixed in the ground (Arthaśāstra 2.3.26), or else a mountain in the Himalayas. The warrior Arjuna, journeying beyond the Gandhamādana, is stopped at the Indrakīla mountain by Sakra/Indra disguised as a brahmin ascetic, at whose urging Arjuna undertakes asceticism in order to gain the power he would need (Mahābhārata 3.3.37). The symbolic sense of “stopping” relates to the Pali sense of immovability, and suggests that the underlying metaphor is the irresistible power represented by Indra.
comment_text.sn1.37:7.4: The primary sense of nāga seems to be “snake”, especially the cobra. From there it applies to other powerful beings: the elephant, the dragon, and as here, the spiritual “giants” of the Saṅgha.

root_text.sn1.37:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sakkesu viharati kapilavatthusmiṁ mahāvane mahatā bhikkhusaṅghena saddhiṁ pañcamattehi bhikkhusatehi sabbeheva arahantehi;
root_text.sn1.37:1.3: dasahi ca lokadhātūhi devatā yebhuyyena sannipatitā honti bhagavantaṁ dassanāya bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn1.37:1.4: Atha kho catunnaṁ suddhāvāsakāyikānaṁ devatānaṁ etadahosi:
root_text.sn1.37:1.5: “ayaṁ kho bhagavā sakkesu viharati kapilavatthusmiṁ mahāvane mahatā bhikkhusaṅghena saddhiṁ pañcamattehi bhikkhusatehi sabbeheva arahantehi;
root_text.sn1.37:1.6: dasahi ca lokadhātūhi devatā yebhuyyena sannipatitā honti bhagavantaṁ dassanāya bhikkhusaṅghañca.
root_text.sn1.37:1.7: Yannūna mayampi yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkameyyāma; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavato santike paccekaṁ gāthaṁ bhāseyyāmā”ti.
root_text.sn1.37:2.1: Atha kho tā devatā—seyyathāpi nāma balavā puriso samiñjitaṁ vā bāhaṁ pasāreyya pasāritaṁ vā bāhaṁ samiñjeyya; evameva—suddhāvāsesu devesu antarahitā bhagavato purato pāturahesuṁ.
root_text.sn1.37:2.2: Atha kho tā devatā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhaṁsu.
root_text.sn1.37:2.3: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitā kho ekā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn1.37:4.1: Atha kho aparā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn1.37:6.1: Atha kho aparā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn1.37:8.1: Atha kho aparā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:

translation_text.sn1.37:0.2: 4. The Host of a Hundred Felicities
translation_text.sn1.37:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, in the Great Wood near Kapilavatthu, together with a large Saṅgha of five hundred mendicants, all of whom were perfected ones.
translation_text.sn1.37:1.3: And most of the deities from ten solar systems had gathered to see the Buddha and the Saṅgha of mendicants.
translation_text.sn1.37:1.4: Then four deities of the Pure Abodes, aware of what was happening, thought:
translation_text.sn1.37:1.7: “Why don’t we go to the Buddha and each recite a verse in his presence?”
translation_text.sn1.37:2.1: Then, as easily as a strong person would extend or contract their arm, they vanished from the Pure Abodes and reappeared in front of the Buddha.
translation_text.sn1.37:2.2: They bowed to the Buddha and stood to one side.
translation_text.sn1.37:2.3: Standing to one side, one deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.37:3.1: “There’s a great congregation in the woods,
translation_text.sn1.37:3.2: where hosts of gods have assembled.
translation_text.sn1.37:3.3: We’ve come to this righteous congregation
translation_text.sn1.37:4.1: Then another deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.37:5.3: Like a charioteer holding the reins,
translation_text.sn1.37:5.4: the astute ones protect their senses.”
translation_text.sn1.37:6.1: Then another deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.37:7.1: “They snapped the post and snapped the cross-bar,
translation_text.sn1.37:7.2: unstirred, they tore out Indra’s pillar.
translation_text.sn1.37:7.4: the young giants tamed by the Clear-eyed One.”
translation_text.sn1.37:8.1: Then another deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.37:9.1: “Anyone who has gone to the Buddha for refuge
translation_text.sn1.37:9.2: won’t go to a plane of loss.
translation_text.sn1.37:9.3: After giving up this human body,
translation_text.sn1.37:9.4: they swell the hosts of gods.”

variant_text.sn1.37:1.7: paccekaṁ gāthaṁ → paccekagāthaṁ (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); paccekagāthā (sya-all, km)
variant_text.sn1.37:5.2: ujukaṁ akaṁsu → ujukamakaṁsu (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)

reference_text.sn1.37:1.1: bj13.48, dr15.33, ms12S1_205, msdiv37, pts-vp-pli1ed1.26, pts-vp-pli2ed1.55
reference_text.sn1.37:2.1: cck15.35, csp1ed12.25, csp2ed12.25, ms12S1_206, pts-vp-pli2ed1.56, sya15.37, vri23.31
